claims.claim_text: Strait of Hormuz effectively closed to US/Israel-linked vessels
claims.notes: High geopolitical significance. Closure would have major economic impact. Source: @CBSNews. | China's statement that 'Our ships are moving in and out of the waters of the Strait of Hormuz. We have trade & energy agreements with Iran. Iran controls the Strait of Hormuz, and it is open for us' directly contests the claim that the Strait is effectively closed. | Web search confirmed the critical importance of the Strait of Hormuz and explicitly mentioned 'False Claims and Denials' regarding activities there, including Iran denying imposing tolls and calling such claims disinformation. This reinforces the 'contested' status and highlights the role of disinformation. | Web search confirms a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz is contributing to fuel shortages, adding weight to the 'effectively closed' side for some entities, but China's statement still contests this for others. | The Philippines seeking US permission to buy Russian crude due to fuel shortages and a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz suggests the Strait is effectively closed for some, but China's prior statement still contests this for others. This reinforces the 'contested' status. | Newsmax reports on Sen. Roger Marshall supporting Trump's blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, framing it as a strategic move. This reinforces the narrative of a blockade, adding to the contested nature of the claim. | Newsmax reports on Sen. Roger Marshall supporting Trump's blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, framing it as a strategic move. This reinforces the narrative of a blockade, adding to the contested nature of the claim. | Web search for 'Strait of Hormuz geopolitical impact 2026' confirms significant instability and a partial or near-total blockade by Iran, disrupting global energy markets. It also states Iran uses its control as leverage and selectively allows passage. China's warning about its interests in the Strait and continued vessel movement (from @Eng_china5) directly contests the idea of a universal closure. The Philippines seeking US approval for Russian oil due to fuel crisis (from @ShaykhSulaiman and @BRICSinfo) further suggests the Strait is effectively closed for some entities, reinforcing the contested nature. | Web search confirms significant instability and partial/near-total blockade in Strait of Hormuz, but China asserts continued access; Philippines seeking US approval for Russian oil due to fuel crisis further reinforces the contested nature for different entities. | Web searches confirm significant escalation in the Strait of Hormuz, including a US naval blockade on Iranian ports and Iran's threats of closure to US/Israel-linked vessels. Shipping is disrupted, and oil prices are surging. However, China asserts continued access for its vessels, and US mine clearance operations are ongoing, indicating the Strait is not universally closed. This reinforces the 'contested' status. | China's warning (from @Eng_china5) asserting continued vessel movement through the Strait of Hormuz, and the Philippines seeking US approval for Russian oil due to fuel shortages, reinforce the contested nature of this claim. Web search also confirms significant instability and partial/near-total blockade but with China asserting continued access. | Web search confirms significant geopolitical escalation in Strait of Hormuz in 2026, with a US naval blockade on Iranian ports and Iran's threats of closure to US/Israel-linked vessels. Shipping is disrupted, and oil prices are surging. However, China asserts continued access for its vessels, and US mine clearance operations are ongoing, indicating the Strait is not universally closed. This reinforces the 'contested' status. | China's warning (from @Eng_china5) asserting continued vessel movement through the Strait of Hormuz, and the Philippines seeking US approval for Russian oil due to fuel shortages, reinforce the contested nature of this claim. Web search also confirms significant instability and partial/near-total blockade but with China asserting continued access. | Fox News report via @atrupar indicates US blockade costing Iran $435M/day, reinforcing the contested nature of the Strait of Hormuz closure for certain entities. | Posts from @ShaykhSulaiman and @maddenifico indicate that some vessels (specifically non-US/Israel linked) are still traversing the Strait of Hormuz. Web searches confirm significant disruption and 'near-complete obstruction' for some, but imply continued, albeit selective, traffic, reinforcing the contested nature of a universal closure.
